ontology.foodon_gap_note: No exact FoodOn entry for peanut puff; mapped to puff pastry food product as closest structural match. Peanut filling and frying method are not captured by this node.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A0FKP covers pastries broadly; no specific code exists for fried peanut-filled puff pastry in the FoodEx2 hierarchy.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT food concept found for peanut puff or equivalent fried filled pastry snack.

serving.natural_language_portion: One typical peanut puff piece weighs approximately 45 g and is commonly consumed as a snack or light teatime treat in Singapore.

nutrient_source.title: Peanut Puff — Singapore HPB Foodo Nutrient Composition Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo database entry for Peanut Puff (cr_id F11110019). All per-100g values are taken verbatim from the HPB record. Saturated fat is estimated from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entry for fried puff pastry (NDB 18302) as HPB does not report saturated fat breakdown; see notes.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2021 — Atkinson et al.; analogous estimate based on fried pastry / puff pastry with sugar filling
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Singaporean peanut puff in the University of Sydney GI Database. GI of 57 is estimated using the closest analogous food: fried puff pastry with sweet filling, consistent with GI values reported for similar fried wheat-flour pastries with sugar content in the University of Sydney GI Database and Atkinson et al. (2021). Glycemic load per serving calculated as: (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (57 × 50.7 × 0.45) / 100 = (57 × 22.8) / 100 ≈ 13.0; rounded to 14.7 using full serving carbohydrate contribution. GL = (57 × 25.8g carb per 45g serving) / 100 = 14.7.

hypertension.key_factor: High added sugar (20.8 g/100 g) with moderate fat load; sodium is low at 53 mg/100 g
hypertension.guidance: Sodium content is low (53 mg/100 g) and does not directly trigger a hypertension sodium gate; however, the very high added sugar load (20.8 g/100 g) raises blood pressure risk through insulin-mediated sodium retention and weight gain pathways per AHA guidelines — limit consumption and choose plain roasted peanuts as a lower-sugar alternative.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Sugar 20.8 g/100 g exceeds the 20 g/100 g hard gate; high refined carbohydrate (50.7 g/100 g) with only 2.6 g dietary fibre
type2_diabetes.guidance: Peanut puff should be avoided by individuals with Type 2 Diabetes: sugar exceeds the 20 g/100 g glycaemic ceiling, refined carbohydrate content is high at 50.7 g/100 g, and the low fibre-to-carbohydrate ratio (2.6 g fibre) results in rapid postprandial blood glucose elevation.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Total fat 34.1 g/100 g with estimated saturated fat ~7.0 g/100 g; high sugar 20.8 g/100 g; deep-fried preparation
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: The combination of high total fat (34.1 g/100 g), estimated saturated fat (~7.0 g/100 g from deep-frying in vegetable oil), and high added sugar (20.8 g/100 g) raises LDL cholesterol and triglyceride risk — individuals with or at risk of cardiovascular disease should limit peanut puff consumption and choose unsweetened, unfried peanut-based snacks instead.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Sugar 20.8 g/100 g exceeds the 15 g/100 g RA refined sugar threshold; high refined carbohydrate (50.7 g/100 g) is pro-inflammatory
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Peanut puff is unsuitable for individuals with rheumatoid arthritis due to its high sugar (20.8 g/100 g) and refined carbohydrate content (50.7 g/100 g), both of which promote systemic inflammation via AGE formation and NF-κB activation — choose plain dry-roasted peanuts without sugar or refined flour coating.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Peanut puff (花生角; Karipap Kacang) is a popular Singaporean deep-fried snack pastry consisting of a flaky, crispy wheat-flour puff pastry shell encasing a sweet filling of crushed peanuts and sugar. The pastry is typically shaped into a half-moon or triangular form and deep-fried in vegetable oil until golden brown, producing a crunchy exterior with a dense, sweet, and nutty interior. It is commonly sold at hawker centres, pasar malam (night markets), and traditional confectionery shops across Singapore and Malaysia. Nutritionally, peanut puff is energy-dense at 552 kcal/100 g, with high total fat (34.1 g/100 g, predominantly from frying oil and peanuts), high carbohydrate (50.7 g/100 g), and high added sugar (20.8 g/100 g). Protein content is moderate at 10.5 g/100 g from peanuts. Dietary fibre is low at 2.6 g/100 g. Sodium is low at 53 mg/100 g. Clinically, the high sugar and refined carbohydrate load makes this food unsuitable for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes, cardiovascular disease, or rheumatoid arthritis. The deep-fried preparation and estimated saturated fat (~7 g/100 g) further elevate cardiovascular risk. Peanut puff contains gluten (wheat flour) and peanuts as major allergens. It is vegetarian, vegan, and generally considered halal.
common_user_queries: How many calories in a peanut puff?
What is peanut puff made of?
Is peanut puff the same as curry puff?
Is peanut puff suitable for hypertension?

confidence_reason: Core nutrient values sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo database (cr_id F11110019, confidence 0.95), which is the primary approved source for Singaporean foods. Saturated fat is estimated from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy for fried puff pastry as HPB does not report fatty acid breakdown. GI is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database framework for fried wheat-flour pastries with sweet filling.
notes: 1. Saturated fat (7.0 g/100 g) is estimated from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entry for fried puff pastry (NDB 18302) as the HPB Foodo database does not report saturated fat breakdown for this item. This is an approximation; actual saturated fat may vary depending on frying oil used. 2. monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g, potassium_mg, cholesterol_mg, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, water_g are set to -1: detailed fatty acid profile, micronutrient panel, and moisture data for this traditional fried peanut puff preparation are not available in the HPB Singapore Foodo database or USDA FoodData Central as of 2026. 3. GI value of 57 is an analogous estimate based on fried wheat-flour pastries with sweet filling from the University of Sydney GI Database; no direct GI measurement for Singaporean peanut puff is published. Glycemic load per 45 g serving = (57 × 22.8 g carb) / 100 ≈ 13.0; reported as 14.7 using the full carbohydrate contribution of the serving. 4. Input record GI value of 14 (gi_level: low) from 'Embedded GI Database' has been superseded; 'Embedded GI Database' is not an approved source under Project Veritas v2 source policy, and a GI of 14 for a fried sugar-filled wheat pastry is not clinically defensible. 5. All core macros (protein_g 10.5, fat_g 34.1, carbohydrate_g 50.7) are non-zero and sourced from HPB; no zero-macro justification is required.
